description: You are the release-manager for a small CLI application that exists in two versions, v1 and v2. Both versions are already checked-out for you in the following fixed directory layout: • /home/user/app/v1/process_data.sh • /home/user/app/v2/process_data.sh Your goal is to produce a repeatable micro-benchmark that compares the two scripts and stores the timing results in a single CSV file that other tools in the release pipeline can consume automatically. Required steps (high level) 1. Run each script three separate times (total of six executions). Use the GNU time utility (/usr/bin/time, not the bash built-in) to capture *real*, *user* and *sys* time of every single run. All six runs may be executed sequentially or in parallel; whatever you choose, the resulting log must contain one line per run. 2. Aggregate the raw numbers into exactly one CSV file named /home/user/deploy/perf_results.csv The parent directory /home/user/deploy does not yet exist — create it with default permissions. 3. The CSV file **must follow this precise format** (no extra spaces, tabs or quotes): • The first line is an exact header line: version,run,real_sec,user_sec,sys_sec • Lines 2-7 (six data lines) follow this grammar: v,,,, where – is either 1 or 2 (representing v1 or v2) – is 1, 2 or 3, reflecting the execution order **inside each version**; the index must restart at 1 when you switch to the next version. – Times are written as decimal seconds with the same precision that /usr/bin/time -f '%e' outputs (typically 1–3 decimal places, e.g. 0.123). No units, just the number. Example of a syntactically correct file (values differ on every machine): version,run,real_sec,user_sec,sys_sec v1,1,1.043,0.002,0.004 v1,2,1.029,0.001,0.003 v1,3,1.037,0.002,0.004 v2,1,0.258,0.000,0.001 v2,2,0.251,0.000,0.001 v2,3,0.254,0.000,0.001 4. When the CSV is complete, print its content to STDOUT so that a human reviewer can see the final results. Additional technical constraints • Do not use sudo. • Do not modify the existing source scripts except to make them executable if necessary. • Use only standard GNU utilities already available in a typical container (bash, awk, sed, sort, etc.). • Make sure the CSV lines appear in the order shown above (header, then three v1 lines, then three v2 lines). What will be automatically tested 1. The directory /home/user/deploy exists. 2. The file /home/user/deploy/perf_results.csv exists. 3. First line is exactly “version,run,real_sec,user_sec,sys_sec”. 4. Lines 2-7 match these six regular expressions **in order**: v1,1,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+ v1,2,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+ v1,3,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+ v2,1,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+ v2,2,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+ v2,3,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+ 5. There are exactly seven lines in the file (no blank trailing lines). Hints (not mandatory) • You may find the format string “/usr/bin/time -f 'real=%e user=%U sys=%S'” useful, then parse with awk -F'[ =]' or similar. • To run two commands in parallel and wait for both, recall background jobs (&) plus wait. • Create the CSV incrementally or build a temporary log and transform it later; the test only cares about the final CSV. • Remember to redirect STDERR (where time writes) to capture it. Good luck!
truth:
Initial filesystem state before the task starts:

1. /home/user/app directory tree:
   /home/user/app/v1/process_data.sh
      Contents:
      #!/usr/bin/env bash
      echo "Processing data – v1"
      sleep 1

   /home/user/app/v2/process_data.sh
      Contents:
      #!/usr/bin/env bash
      echo "Processing data – v2"
      sleep 0.25

   Both files have Unix line endings and are executable (chmod 755).

2. No /home/user/deploy directory exists yet.

Expected end state after the agent finishes:

1. Directory /home/user/deploy (permissions 755, owned by user).

2. File /home/user/deploy/perf_results.csv with exactly 7 non-empty lines:
   Line 1 (header):
     version,run,real_sec,user_sec,sys_sec

   Lines 2-4:
     v1,1,<float>,<float>,<float>
     v1,2,<float>,<float>,<float>
     v1,3,<float>,<float>,<float>

   Lines 5-7:
     v2,1,<float>,<float>,<float>
     v2,2,<float>,<float>,<float>
     v2,3,<float>,<float>,<float>

   <float> matches regular expression [0-9]+\.[0-9]+
   No trailing commas, no spaces.

3. perf_results.csv line order exactly as above; newline at end of file optional.

Automated checker logic will:
 • Confirm the file pathname.
 • Read first line for exact header.
 • Count total lines == 7.
 • Validate each data line against its regex.
The actual numeric values are not fixed and therefore are not part of the ground truth.
